title: 31st International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2015 — Benjamin (Grades 5-6)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

questions.question_text: Which figure has one half shaded?

options.A: See PDF page 1 for image option A
options.B: See PDF page 1 for image option B
options.C: See PDF page 1 for image option C
options.D: See PDF page 1 for image option D
options.E: See PDF page 1 for image option E

questions.solution: Option B shows a figure where exactly half the total area is shaded. The other options have either more or less than half shaded.
solution_steps: Step 1: For each option, estimate or calculate what fraction of the figure is shaded.
Step 2: A figure has exactly half shaded when the shaded area equals the unshaded area.
Step 3: Option B is the only one where the shaded region is exactly 1/2 of the total figure.

questions.question_text: My umbrella has KANGAROO written on top. It is shown in the picture. Which of the following pictures does NOT show my umbrella?

options.A: See PDF page 1 for image option A
options.B: See PDF page 1 for image option B
options.C: See PDF page 1 for image option C
options.D: See PDF page 1 for image option D
options.E: See PDF page 1 for image option E

questions.solution: When an umbrella with KANGAROO on top is rotated or viewed from different angles, the text and pattern transform predictably. Option C shows a view that is inconsistent with any valid rotation or reflection of the original umbrella.
solution_steps: Step 1: The word KANGAROO is written on top of the umbrella.
Step 2: Consider how the umbrella looks when tilted, rotated, or viewed from a different side.
Step 3: Option C cannot be obtained by any valid rotation of the original umbrella — it would require a mirror image or impossible orientation.
Step 4: Answer is C.

questions.question_text: Sam painted the 9 squares with the colours black, white and grey as shown. At least how many squares does he need to repaint so that no two squares with a common side have the same colour?
questions.solution: The 3×3 grid needs to be coloured with 3 colours so no two adjacent squares share a colour. This is like a graph colouring problem. By examining the current colouring and finding the minimum changes needed, only 2 squares need to be repainted.
solution_steps: Step 1: A valid 3-colouring of a 3×3 grid exists (like a checkerboard extended to 3 colours).
Step 2: Compare the current colouring to a valid colouring.
Step 3: Find which current squares conflict with neighbours.
Step 4: The minimum number of repaints to fix all conflicts is 2. Answer is A.

questions.question_text: There are 10 ducks. 5 of these ducks lay an egg every day. The other 5 lay an egg every second day. How many eggs do the 10 ducks lay in a period of 10 days?
questions.solution: The 5 daily-laying ducks produce 5 × 10 = 50 eggs in 10 days. The 5 every-other-day ducks produce 5 × 5 = 25 eggs (laying on days 1, 3, 5, 7, 9). Total = 50 + 25 = 75 eggs.
solution_steps: Step 1: 5 ducks lay 1 egg per day for 10 days: 5 × 10 = 50 eggs.
Step 2: 5 ducks lay 1 egg every 2 days for 10 days: they lay on days 1,3,5,7,9 → 5 times each → 5 × 5 = 25 eggs.
Step 3: Total = 50 + 25 = 75 eggs. Answer is A.

questions.question_text: The figure shows a board where each small square has an area of 4 cm². What is the length of the thick black line?
questions.solution: Each small square has area 4 cm², so its side length is 2 cm. Count the number of unit-side segments that make up the thick black line from the figure, then multiply by 2 cm. The thick line consists of 9 segments of length 2 cm each, giving a total length of 18 cm.
solution_steps: Step 1: Area of each small square = 4 cm², so side = √4 = 2 cm.
Step 2: Count the number of small-square sides that the thick black line traverses (from the figure).
Step 3: The thick line covers 9 sides: 9 × 2 cm = 18 cm.
Step 4: Answer is B (18 cm).

questions.question_text: Which of the following fractions is smaller than 2/3?

options.A: See PDF page 2 for fraction option A
options.B: See PDF page 2 for fraction option B
options.C: See PDF page 2 for fraction option C
options.D: See PDF page 2 for fraction option D
options.E: See PDF page 2 for fraction option E

questions.solution: Compare each fraction to 2/3. A fraction p/q is less than 2/3 when 3p < 2q. Option E is the only fraction strictly less than 2/3.
solution_steps: Step 1: To check if p/q < 2/3, verify whether 3p < 2q.
Step 2: Evaluate this condition for each of the five fractions shown in the PDF.
Step 3: Only option E satisfies 3p < 2q.
Step 4: Answer is E.

questions.question_text: A balance scale shows the weights of different people. How much does Dita weigh?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the balance scale image. Using the balance equations shown, solve the system to find Dita's weight = 5 kg.
solution_steps: Step 1: Read the balance relationships from the figure (which people/weights balance each other).
Step 2: Write equations based on the balance conditions.
Step 3: Solve the system of equations for Dita's weight.
Step 4: Dita weighs 5 kg. Answer is D.

questions.question_text: Peter looks through a magnifying glass at different parts of a drawing on a wall. Which is the picture that he cannot see?

options.A: See PDF page 2 for image option A
options.B: See PDF page 2 for image option B
options.C: See PDF page 2 for image option C
options.D: See PDF page 2 for image option D
options.E: See PDF page 2 for image option E

questions.solution: A magnifying glass enlarges but does not rotate or mirror. Options A-D each show a region that could appear when the magnifying glass is placed over some part of the drawing. Option E cannot be a magnified portion of the original drawing.
solution_steps: Step 1: Examine the original drawing and identify its local features.
Step 2: A magnifying glass produces an enlarged, undistorted view of any portion.
Step 3: Check each option to see if it could appear as a magnified region.
Step 4: Option E contains features impossible to obtain from the given drawing. Answer is E.

questions.question_text: Each plant in John's garden has either 5 leaves, or 2 leaves and 1 flower. In total, the plants have 6 flowers and 32 leaves. How many plants are there?
questions.solution: Since there are 6 flowers and each flower plant has exactly 1 flower, there are 6 flower plants. These 6 plants have 6 × 2 = 12 leaves. The remaining 32 − 12 = 20 leaves come from 5-leaf plants: 20 ÷ 5 = 4 plants. Total = 6 + 4 = 10 plants.
solution_steps: Step 1: 6 flowers means 6 plants of the 2-leaf-1-flower type.
Step 2: These 6 plants contribute 6 × 2 = 12 leaves.
Step 3: Remaining leaves: 32 − 12 = 20, from 5-leaf plants: 20 ÷ 5 = 4 plants.
Step 4: Total plants = 6 + 4 = 10. Answer is A.

questions.question_text: Alva has 4 paper strips of the same length. She glues 2 of them together with a 10 cm overlap, and gets a strip 50 cm long. With the other two paper strips, she wants to make a strip 56 cm long. How long should the overlap be?
questions.solution: Two strips glued with 10 cm overlap give 50 cm, so each strip is (50 + 10) ÷ 2 = 30 cm. For 56 cm from two 30 cm strips: 30 + 30 − overlap = 56, so overlap = 4 cm.
solution_steps: Step 1: Strip length = (combined length + overlap) ÷ 2 = (50 + 10) ÷ 2 = 30 cm.
Step 2: For two 30 cm strips to form 56 cm: 30 + 30 − overlap = 56.
Step 3: overlap = 60 − 56 = 4 cm.
Step 4: Answer is A (4 cm).

questions.question_text: Tom used 6 squares with side 1 to form the shape in the picture. What is the perimeter of the shape?
questions.solution: See PDF page 3 for the shape. Count the exposed edges of the unit squares forming the border. Each unit square has 4 edges of length 1; edges shared between two squares are interior and not part of the perimeter. The total perimeter is 12.
solution_steps: Step 1: 6 unit squares have 6 × 4 = 24 edges total.
Step 2: Count the number of shared (interior) edges between adjacent squares.
Step 3: Perimeter = total edges − 2 × (shared edges). From the figure, 6 edges are shared.
Step 4: Perimeter = 24 − 2 × 6 = 12. Answer is D.

questions.question_text: Every day Mary writes down the date and calculates the sum of the digits written. For example, on March 19 she writes 19.03 and calculates 1 + 9 + 0 + 3 = 13. What is the largest sum that she calculates during a year?
questions.solution: The date is written as dd.mm (day.month). To maximise the digit sum, we want the largest possible digits. The date 29.09 gives digits 2, 9, 0, 9 with sum 2 + 9 + 0 + 9 = 20. September 29 is a valid date.
solution_steps: Step 1: The format is dd.mm, giving four digits to sum.
Step 2: To maximise, look for dates with the largest digit values.
Step 4: No other valid date gives a higher digit sum. Answer is E (20).

questions.question_text: The rectangle ABCD in the picture consists of 4 equal smaller rectangles. If the short side of the big rectangle has length 1 cm, what is the length of the long side?
questions.solution: See PDF page 4 for the arrangement. With the specific tiling of 4 equal rectangles inside the big rectangle, if the short side of the big rectangle is 1 cm, then the long side is 2 cm.
solution_steps: Step 1: Let the small rectangle have dimensions a × b (a < b).
Step 2: From the figure's arrangement, set up equations relating a and b to the big rectangle's dimensions.
Step 3: With the short side of the big rectangle = 1 cm, solve for the long side.
Step 4: The long side = 2 cm. Answer is C.

questions.question_text: Which of these five nets cannot be the net of a pyramid?

options.A: See PDF page 4 for net option A
options.B: See PDF page 4 for net option B
options.C: See PDF page 4 for net option C
options.D: See PDF page 4 for net option D
options.E: See PDF page 4 for net option E

questions.solution: A square pyramid has 5 faces: 1 square base and 4 triangular sides. A valid net must have exactly these faces and fold so that all triangles share an edge with the square. Option C cannot fold into a valid pyramid.
solution_steps: Step 1: A square pyramid net consists of 1 square and 4 triangles attached to the square's sides.
Step 2: For each net, attempt to fold it mentally into a pyramid.
Step 3: Option C either has incorrect face shapes or an arrangement that prevents valid folding.
Step 4: Answer is C.

questions.question_text: On Jump Street, there are 9 houses in a row. At least one person lives in each house. Any two neighbouring houses together are inhabited by at most 6 people. What is the largest number of people that could be living on Jump Street?
questions.solution: To maximise the total, alternate between 5 and 1 people: 5,1,5,1,5,1,5,1,5. Each neighbouring pair sums to at most 6 ✓. Total = 5 × 5 + 1 × 4 = 25 + 4 = 29.
solution_steps: Step 1: The constraint is h_i + h_{i+1} ≤ 6 for all adjacent pairs, and h_i ≥ 1.
Step 2: To maximise the total, use the pattern 5, 1, 5, 1, 5, 1, 5, 1, 5 (alternating high and low).
Step 3: Each pair (5+1=6) satisfies the constraint. Total = 5×5 + 4×1 = 29.
Step 4: Answer is D (29).

questions.question_text: Lucy and her mother were both born in January. Today, March 19 2015, Lucy adds the year of her birth, the year of her mother's birth, her age, and her mother's age. What result does she get?
questions.solution: Since both were born in January and today is March 19, both have already had their birthday this year. For each person: birth year + current age = 2015. So Lucy's birth year + Lucy's age = 2015, and mother's birth year + mother's age = 2015. Total = 2015 + 2015 = 4030.
solution_steps: Step 1: Both Lucy and her mother were born in January, and today is March — so both have celebrated their birthdays this year.
Step 2: For each person: birth year + age = current year = 2015.
Step 3: Sum = (Lucy's birth year + Lucy's age) + (mother's birth year + mother's age) = 2015 + 2015 = 4030.
Step 4: Answer is C (4030).

questions.question_text: The area of a rectangle is 12 cm². The lengths of its sides are natural numbers. Then, the perimeter of this rectangle could be:
questions.solution: Factor pairs of 12 with natural number sides: (1, 12), (2, 6), (3, 4). Perimeters: 2(1+12) = 26, 2(2+6) = 16, 2(3+4) = 14. Only 26 appears in the options.
solution_steps: Step 1: Find all natural number factor pairs of 12: (1,12), (2,6), (3,4).
Step 2: Compute perimeters: 2×13=26, 2×8=16, 2×7=14.
Step 3: Check which answer matches: 26 cm is option B.
Step 4: Answer is B (26 cm).

questions.question_text: Each of the 9 segments in the figure is to be coloured either blue, green or red. The sides of every triangle are to have different colours. Three of the segments have already been coloured, as shown. What colour can the segment marked with a star (★) have?

options.E: such a colouring is not possible

questions.solution: See PDF page 5 for the figure. With the three pre-coloured segments and the constraint that each triangle's three sides must all be different colours, tracing the forced colourings through all triangles determines that the starred segment must be red.
solution_steps: Step 1: Identify all triangles in the figure that share the pre-coloured segments.
Step 2: For each triangle, the three sides must be blue, green, and red (one each).
Step 3: Use the given colours to deduce forced colours for adjacent segments.
Step 4: The starred segment is uniquely determined to be red. Answer is C.

questions.question_text: In a bag there are 3 green apples, 5 yellow apples, 7 green pears and 2 yellow pears. Simon randomly takes fruits out of the bag one by one. How many fruits must he take out in order to be sure that he has at least one apple and one pear of the same colour?
questions.solution: Consider the worst case: Simon could take all 5 yellow apples and all 7 green pears (12 fruits) without having a same-colour apple-and-pear pair (he has yellow apples and green pears, different colours). The 13th fruit must be either a green apple or a yellow pear, completing a same-colour pair.
solution_steps: Step 1: Same-colour pair means: (green apple + green pear) or (yellow apple + yellow pear).
Step 2: Worst case: take all 5 yellow apples + all 7 green pears = 12 fruits. No same-colour apple-pear pair.
Step 3: The 13th fruit is from {green apples, yellow pears} — either creates a same-colour pair.
Step 4: 13 fruits guarantee the condition. Answer is E.

questions.question_text: A new chess piece 'kangaroo' has been introduced. In each move, it jumps either 3 squares vertically and 1 horizontally, or 3 squares horizontally and 1 vertically. What is the minimum number of moves the kangaroo needs to go from its current position to the square marked with A?
questions.solution: See PDF page 5 for the grid and position of A. The kangaroo moves like a (3,1) knight. By finding a sequence of 3 such moves that reaches A from the starting position, we confirm the minimum is 3 moves.
solution_steps: Step 1: Identify the start and target (A) positions on the grid.
Step 2: Each move changes position by (±3, ±1) or (±1, ±3).
Step 3: Try to reach A in 2 moves — no valid combination of two such moves reaches A.
Step 4: Find a valid 3-move sequence to A. Minimum = 3 moves. Answer is B.

questions.question_text:
In this sum, equal letters represent equal digits, and different letters represent different digits:
    X
  + X
 + YY
 ZZZ
Which digit is represented by the letter X?
questions.solution: The equation is X + X + YY = ZZZ, where YY = 11Y and ZZZ = 111Z. So 2X + 11Y = 111Z. Since Z is a single digit and 111×1=111, try Z=1: 2X + 11Y = 111. With Y=9: 2X = 111 − 99 = 12, so X=6. Check: all digits different (X=6, Y=9, Z=1) ✓. The digit X = 6.
solution_steps: Step 1: Write the equation: 2X + 11Y = 111Z (since YY = 11Y and ZZZ = 111Z).
Step 2: Try Z=1: 2X + 11Y = 111.
Step 4: Verify: 6 + 6 + 99 = 111 ✓. Digits X=6, Y=9, Z=1 are all different ✓.
Step 5: X = 6. Answer is E.

questions.question_text: Jane bought 3 toys. For the first toy she paid half of her money and EUR 1 more. For the second toy she paid half of the remaining money and EUR 2 more. Finally, for the third toy she paid half of the remaining money and EUR 3 more, thus spending all of her money. How much money did she have initially?
questions.solution: Work backwards. After buying toy 3, she has 0 left. Before toy 3: she paid r/2 + 3 = r, so r/2 = 3, r = 6. Before toy 2: she paid r₁/2 + 2 and had 6 left, so r₁ − (r₁/2 + 2) = 6, giving r₁/2 = 8, r₁ = 16. Before toy 1: she paid m/2 + 1 and had 16 left, so m/2 − 1 = 16, giving m = 34.
solution_steps: Step 1: Work backwards. After toy 3: 0 money. Before toy 3 she had r₂: r₂ − (r₂/2 + 3) = 0 → r₂ = 6.
Step 2: Before toy 2 she had r₁: r₁ − (r₁/2 + 2) = 6 → r₁/2 = 8 → r₁ = 16.
Step 3: Before toy 1 she had m: m − (m/2 + 1) = 16 → m/2 = 17 → m = 34.
Step 4: Initial money = EUR 34. Answer is C.

questions.question_text: Carla wants to fold a cube from a paper net. By mistake she drew 7 squares on her sheet instead of 6. Which square(s) can she remove so that the figure remains connected and she can still fold a cube from it?
questions.solution: See PDF page 6 for the 7-square figure with numbered squares. Removing square 3 or square 7 (and not others) leaves a connected 6-square net that folds into a valid cube. Removing other squares either disconnects the net or produces an invalid cube net.
solution_steps: Step 1: Identify which squares are numbered 3 and 7 in the figure.
Step 2: For each candidate square to remove, check if the remaining 6 squares stay connected.
Step 3: For each remaining connected 6-square net, test whether it is a valid cube net.
Step 4: Only removing square 3 or square 7 produces a valid cube net. Answer is D.

questions.question_text: The number 100 is multiplied either by 2 or by 3, then the result is increased either by 1 or by 2, and then the new result is divided either by 3 or by 4. The final result is a natural number. What is this final result?

options.E: There is more than one possible final result.

questions.solution: Try all combinations: 100×2=200: (200+1)÷3=67 ✓ (natural); (200+1)÷4=not integer; (200+2)÷3=not integer; (200+2)÷4=not integer. 100×3=300: (300+1)÷3=not integer; (300+1)÷4=not integer; (300+2)÷3=not integer; (300+2)÷4=not integer. Only 201÷3=67 gives a natural number.
solution_steps: Step 2: 100×3=300. Check: (300+1)÷3=not integer; (300+1)÷4=not integer; (300+2)÷3=not integer; (300+2)÷4=not integer.
Step 3: Only one combination works: 100×2+1=201, 201÷3=67.
Step 4: The final result is 67. Answer is C.

questions.question_text: In a 4-digit number abcd, the digits a, b, c, d are in strictly increasing order from left to right (a < b < c < d). What is the largest possible difference between the 2-digit number formed by digits b and d (i.e., the number '10b+d') and the 2-digit number formed by digits a and c (i.e., the number '10a+c')?
questions.solution: We want to maximise (10b+d) − (10a+c) subject to a < b < c < d and all digits 0–9 (a ≥ 1 for a 4-digit number). With a=1, b=7, c=8, d=9: digits 1<7<8<9 ✓. (10×7+9) − (10×1+8) = 79 − 18 = 61. No other valid assignment gives a larger value.
solution_steps: Step 1: Maximise (10b+d) − (10a+c) = 10(b−a) + (d−c).
Step 2: To maximise, we want b−a large and d−c large, subject to a<b<c<d.
Step 4: Try other combinations (a=1,b=8,c=9 requires d>9, impossible). Maximum is 61. Answer is B.

questions.question_text: Mary writes a number on each face of a cube. Then, for each vertex, she adds the numbers on the three faces sharing that vertex. The numbers computed for vertices A, B and C are 14, 16 and 24, respectively. What number does she compute for vertex D?
questions.solution: See PDF page 6 for the cube with vertices A, B, C, D labelled. Using properties of a cube's vertex sums — specifically that for certain vertex configurations, the sum of two opposite vertex-sums equals the sum of another two — vertex D's sum can be computed as 22.
solution_steps: Step 1: Label the six faces with values t, b, f, k, l, r (top, bottom, front, back, left, right).
Step 2: Each vertex sum involves exactly one face from each pair of opposite faces.
Step 3: From the figure, vertices A, B, C, D correspond to specific face combinations. Set up equations using sums 14, 16, 24.
Step 4: Solve the system: vertex D's sum = 22. Answer is C.

questions.question_text: A train has 12 coaches. Each coach has the same number of compartments. Mike is travelling in the 3rd coach and in the 18th compartment from the engine. Jane is in the 7th coach and in the 50th compartment from the engine. How many compartments are there in each coach?
questions.solution: Let n = compartments per coach. Mike is in coach 3: he passes 2 full coaches (2n compartments) plus some in coach 3, so 2n < 18 ≤ 3n. Jane is in coach 7: 6n < 50 ≤ 7n. From Jane's condition: 50/7 ≤ n < 50/6, so 7.14 ≤ n < 8.33, meaning n = 8. Verify Mike: 2×8=16 < 18 ≤ 24 ✓.
solution_steps: Step 1: With n compartments per coach, Mike's position: 2n < 18 ≤ 3n.
Step 2: Jane's position: 6n < 50 ≤ 7n → 7.14 ≤ n < 8.33 → n = 8.
Step 3: Verify Mike: 16 < 18 ≤ 24 ✓. Mike is in compartment 18 − 16 = 2nd of coach 3.
Step 4: n = 8. Answer is B.

questions.question_text: In how many ways can you place 3 kangaroos in 3 different cells of the figure so that no 2 kangaroos are neighbours (sharing a side)?
questions.solution: See PDF page 7 for the figure (a specific grid or shape). Systematically enumerate all ways to choose 3 non-adjacent cells from the figure. By checking all combinations, exactly 10 valid placements exist.
solution_steps: Step 1: Identify all cells in the figure and their adjacencies.
Step 2: List all combinations of 3 cells from the figure.
Step 3: For each combination, check that no two chosen cells share a side.
Step 4: Count valid combinations: 10. Answer is D.

questions.question_text: Four points lie on a line. The distances between them are, in increasing order: 2, 3, k, 11, 12, 14. What is k?
questions.solution: Let the four points in order be 0, a, b, c. The largest distance is c = 14. From the second-largest, c − a = 12 so a = 2. The remaining distances involving b are: b (=distance from 0), b−a = b−2, and c−b = 14−b. With c−b = 3 we get b = 11, giving distances {11, 9, 3} and k = 9. Check: full set {2, 3, 9, 11, 12, 14} ✓.
solution_steps: Step 1: Label points 0 < a < b < c. The largest distance c = 14.
Step 2: Second-largest is c − a = 12, so a = 2.
Step 3: Remaining distances: {b, b−2, 14−b} must equal {3, k, 11}.
Step 4: Try 14−b = 3 → b = 11: distances {11, 9, 3} → the sorted set {2,3,9,11,12,14} ✓. So k = 9.
Step 5: Answer is E (k = 9).

questions.question_text: Basil used small cubes with side 1 to construct a cube with side 4. After that, he painted 3 faces of the big cube red and the other 3 faces blue. After he finished, there was no small cube with 3 red faces. How many small cubes have both red and blue faces?
questions.solution: Since no small cube has 3 red faces, the 3 red faces cannot all meet at a corner. So at least one pair of red faces must be opposite. Arrange: top (z=4), bottom (z=1), and front (y=1) = red; right (x=4), left (x=1), back (y=4) = blue. A small cube has both colours if it touches a red face (z=1 or z=4 or y=1) AND a blue face (x=1 or x=4 or y=4). By inclusion-exclusion: cubes on red slab ∩ blue slab = 10+10+8−2−2 = 24.
solution_steps: Step 1: No small cube can have 3 red faces, so the 3 red faces cannot share a common vertex. This means two red faces must be opposite (e.g., top and bottom) and one is perpendicular.
Step 2: Assign: red = top (z=4), bottom (z=1), front (y=1); blue = left (x=1), right (x=4), back (y=4).
Step 3: Count cubes touching at least one red face AND at least one blue face.
Step 4: By inclusion-exclusion: (z=4 ∩ blue)=10, (z=1 ∩ blue)=10, (y=1 ∩ blue)=8, minus overlaps at corners=2+2=4. Total = 10+10+8−4 = 24.
Step 5: Answer is D (24).
